paragraphs.body: Personal Apocalypses Submission 2
My main concern with this section is how the summary is handled. Is it clear what is going on? Is it consise? Interesting? Thanks :)

Mrs. Grant was a known hoarder, which is why, when Hunter told his mom about him and Ant’s summer job, she called her immediately. They were friends, though Hunter’s mom never referred to the forty-five year old widow as such. But they’d grown up together, spent their whole lives in the same place, which made them friends in Hunter’s eyes. He knew from a framed photo in his parents’ room that Mrs. Grant was a bridesmaid at his mom’s wedding, too. Regradless of what they were, Mrs. Grant was happy to have the boys over to clean out her garage.

annotations.comment: I genuinely looked forward to reading your submission! This should be “his”
Is it going to be important to know all of this about Mrs. Grant (maybe!)?

paragraphs.body: “The garage is sort of like my junk drawer” she chided when they arrived in Ant’s minivan. The sixteenth-birthday hand-me-down was their key to success: removable backseats, layers of newspaper and cardboard covering the backset, and noseplugs in the glove department would allow them to take Mrs. Grant’s trash (and anyone else’s) to the dump for a modest fee. On the short drive over they fought over what that fee should be— Hunter was terrified of overcharging people and losing business, and Ant didn’t want to work for below minimum wage— but in the end they agreed that they would just take whatever Mrs. Grant offered them. She met them at the curb and walked them to the driveway.

annotations.comment: Nice summary — enough details to keep it engaging.

paragraphs.body: Even though Hunter and Ant repeatedly assured Mrs. Grant that they would be fine by themselves, she insisted on staying with them for the first hour. To “make sure they didn’t toss anything important.” However, she was no help when it came to opening the garage door, which jammed multiple times as the boys attempted to shove it upwards multiple times. Even before they had it open, they say that the two-car stall was literally filled to the brim, with not enough space even for a toy car, and when the door finally did open with a groan, Ant looked at his companion with concern for the well-being of everyone involved in this project. He squinted his cheeks and reached for a garbage bag at head level. With Hunter’s help he was able to pull it down, but not before a toy cash regiter from god-knows-where fell along with it. They slid back half a step and it slammed into the ground with a ding, half an inch from Ant’s foot. Mrs. Grant ran over, repeating “I’m so sorry” at a low volume. Hunter instinctively replied “It’s alright ma’am” but she was already gone, having taken the cash register and run into the house. She returned empty-handed a few minutes later, and when Ant asked if she was alright she only replied that she didn’t realize how full the garage had gotten.

annotations.context_left: anything important.” However, she was no help when it came to opening the garage door, which jammed
annotations.context_right: as the boys attempted to shove it upwards multiple times. Even before they had it open, they say th

annotations.comment: Yikes! I think I would hide that she’s a hoarder and maybe have Ant and Hunter discuss this once she runs away.

paragraphs.body: After two and a half hours Hunter and Ant could see the celing of the garage, but progress felt much slower than that. Every stack of string-tied magazines or assortment of rusty kitchenware or hardwood drawer that must’ve been beautiful at some point, Mrs. Grant would inevitably remember that it belonged somewhere else and carry it away. Hunter began to purse his lips at her comings and goings as the garage emptied but the pile of everything on the lawn only got smaller. The harsh summer sun and lack of food (Mrs. Grant had promised them lunch) didn’t help the boys’ frustrations.
“What’s the point of cleaning the garage if she throws everything back in the house?” Ant said. He yanked at a golf bag, with one foot on the stack of boxes beside it that pinned it against the wall.

Hunter balanced a handful of children’s blocks in his hands. “I’m sure she needs some of this stuff.” Stepping over a fallen dresser filled with dry cleaning covers, he dumped them on the ground. They landed in a line, spelling out SMLJKZA.
“No way. Look at this stuff. And that easel she brought in was totally broken in half.” Ant replied.
“I bet it’s sentimental.” Hunter whispered. She came back outside and stood with her arms crossed, looking around. Then she noticed the blocks, and her eyes lit up behind her cat-eye glasses.
“Oh, you boys are doing a great job! You know, I was skeptical when your mother called me, Hunter, but thank you so much.”

At being praised he felt a small rosiness rush to his cheeks. He looked at Ant, who just shrugged and continued breaking down cardboard boxes. Mrs. Grant procured a plastic bag from somewhere and began grabbing the toy blocks, one by one. “You know,” she said, bending over, “these belonged to my best friend when we we little. In fact, Karen and the two of us were quite the trio.” Looking at Hunter, who had stopped working to listen, she added, “did your mother ever tell you about that.”
“Not at all.”
She chuckled. “That’s very like her. Well Reese, that was his name, moved away, and when his poor father died he left me with all of his stuff.”

annotations.text: he left me with all of his stuff
annotations.comment: I like the conversation and the connection, but I was confused that the dad of a kid who died would bring his stuff back to a friend in a neighborhood he had moved away from.

paragraphs.body: Hunter flashed a smile at Ant. Not only was he right, but he wanted to know more about his mother’s past. Neither of his parents talked about the past much— well, they didn’t talk much at all recently. All he knew is that his dad grew up in New York, and that they met at a bar when his mom went to see a Broadway show. He turned to Mrs. Grant.
“Why did he move away?”
“Well...” her nostalgic smile faded. “That’s for another time. Tell you what, if you find more of his old stuff, and my memory hasn’t gotten too fuzzy with age, I’ll tell you some stories.”

She went back inside with the blocks and Hunter looked at the garage. It was about half-cleared. Mrs. Grant didn’t come back for a while, and him and Ant got into a flow. Hunter, standing on plastic crates they’d pulled out some time ago that were filled with bundled up receipts, haphazardly threw things onto the lawn, where Ant organized everything into two piles: trash and maybe trash. Neither said much for a while, even when the older woman came back and took three things from the trash and nothing from the maybe trash.

annotations.context_left: s, haphazardly threw things onto the lawn, where Ant organized everything into two piles: trash and
annotations.context_right: trash. Neither said much for a while, even when the older woman came back and took three things fro

paragraphs.body: In fact, after an hour of this they worked without saying a word to each other, their backs to the sun now low in the sky. Ant took short breaks and played games on his phone, Hunter listened to music while he reached and tossed. The only sounds were of cars driving past and the various thuds, crashes and groans of their work. When Mrs. Grant came out Ant no longer payed her any mind, but Hunter would slip his headphones around his neck and they they’d have jilts of conversation about whatever childhood art piece or action figured he’d come across.

He learned a lot about this mysterious figure, Reese. Mrs. Grant told him about how, as kids, they would roam around Lancaster, complaining about school and turning over stones and avoiding their parents until the sun went down. She told him about the basketball courts at the park, the friendly neighbors that would feed them on a whim, and the candy they’d buy at the convience store, shoving it all down their throat before walking in the house. She told him about his mother, how Karen was the least sensible, most fearless one of their trio. Her arms sped around while she told these stories, like she’d die if she was still for even a moment, and all of her tales had way too much backstory.

“We would play in the outskirts of the woods.” She raised her palms over her head, outstretching them between the sky and Hunter’s eyes. “The rule was that if we could see the sun, we were okay. Not that I condone a bunch of kids staring at the sun all day.” They both laughed. “There was a dried up lake that we’d jump across, back and forth, back and forth. It was a test of strength, and we’d make all the neighborhood kids do it. One day Reese got his shoe stuck in the mud, and we fished it out with a stick. We left them out to dry overnight. Well in the morning, Reese goes to put them on and notices a leg, then two. There was a wolf spider! He freaks out, but your mother jumps in and begins shaking the shoe like no tomorrow, and the spider flew out. Like I said, fearless.” She grabbed the tiny shoes by Hunter’s feet and brought them inside, humming as she went.

annotations.comment: I’m slightly confused if this is the same town they’re in now, or not?

annotations.context_right: . One day Reese got his shoe stuck in the mud, and we fished it out with a stick. We left them out t

annotations.comment: Wasn’t quite sure if these tiny shoes were supposed to be Reese’s or not?

paragraphs.body: Tucked away in a corner, covered by spiderwebs and dust and half-dented by lacrosse equipment, was a box. Ant pulled it out, left it on the concrete where is cast a long shadow in the setting sun before rushing off to fill up the car. The garage was about empty, and he looked miserable. It’s the sun, Hunter concluded, before looking on at the small, grey container. The tape holding it shut ripped open like Velcro. He coughed twice, the first into the air and the second into his elbow, as he peered inside to find a stack of letters.

All of the them were addressed to Reese, and were from “K” and “H.” K’s were shorter, had multiple words crossed out and rewritten in each, while H’s were more articulate, with fancy cursive r’s. Both authors signed Dear Reese with a heart. Hunter picked through them, reading snippets of one before getting embarrassed and moving to the next, when suddenly there was a second set of hands reaching inside the box.

“Dear Reese, you looked good in math today. Love, Karen.” Mrs. Grant chuckled to herself, picked up another. “Dear Reese, I saw you from across the playground today helping Mary Rose up after she fell. It was very kind, but I wish you held me the same way. Love, Harriet. God, wasn’t I a poet,” she added. Hunter stayed silent; the only sounds were her rifling and Ant’s belabored grunts. Eventually he mustered, “There’s a lot of these.”

annotations.comment: I really enjoy this. I love that Ant is totally in his own world. I need some help understanding (as I think is correct) that these were letters the girls wrote together but never meant for Reese to get?

paragraphs.body: “There are,” Mrs. Grant replied.
“You gave all of these to him?”
“He found them.”
“What happened?”

“Oh, nothing bad.” She paused for a second as her eyes darkened. A gust of wind swept the street, and the heaps of black grabage bags on lining the curb whispered in unison. She was staring at Hunter, almost studying him. “Like I said, he moved away. We were devastated, but kids move on quickly. I wish I’d kept in touch with his father, though.”

annotations.text: in touch with his father, though.”
annotations.comment: I can’t tell if she means this or what.

paragraphs.body: She stood up and put her hands on her lower back to stretch. Hunter thought he saw a tear on her cheek, but he wrote it off as a trick of the evening’s dying light.
“Well, I’ll get out of your hair so you can finish up. Thank you boys for doing this, I’m going to recommend you to everyone at my book club tomorrow.”

“Thank you!” Hunter replied. He turned around to find the driveway mostly empty, the car mostly packed up. He was surprised, but only shrugged and dragged the plastic cabinet he’d been sitting on over to his friend. He tried to force open the two drawers. The first one was jammed, but the second flew off with a tiny bit of force. There wasn’t anything inside.
“Definitely trash” he shouted to his friend.

Ant didn’t reply, just grabbed the cabinet and threw it into the car. He’d taken his earbuds out, and his back was drenched in sweat. It took ten more minutes to empty the garage and fill up the car, during which neither of them said a word.

When they finished Mrs. Grant paid them fifty dollars each, two crisp bills that sat in between them as they pulled out of her driveway. Ant’s rage seemed to be simmering, but no conversation topic, not power scaling or game strategy or Cece, would revive his mood. Hunter’s anxious mind ran, intensified by his friend’s refusal to turn up his music past a whisper. They stopped at a gas station, Hunter got chips, and offered offered some to Ant who refused, and then they were on the highway. After five minutes, Ant finally spoke.

annotations.comment: Word choice is strange and give them a sentence to get into the car.

paragraphs.body: “You know that garage is gonna be filled up again in a week.”
“What?” Hunter was scrolling through social media.
“She took all that stuff inside for no reason.”
“Yeah?”
“So we worked all day for nothing. In eighty-five degree weather.”

“You were in the garage the whole time, what sun?” He meant it as a joke, but it came out snarky. He was thirsty, he should’ve gotten a drink. The song changed, and Hunter tried again to reach for the volume knob. Ant took his eyes off the road, watched him do it, turned it back down again.
“Sorry, I was doing all the work while you were having tea time” Ant said.
“Tea time?”
“Tea time, Hunter.” He repeated himself, staring directly ahead. “Tea time. I did all the work.”

annotations.comment: I love the stupidity of this phrase and how he clings to it.
annotations.context_right: , Hunter.” He repeated himself, staring directly ahead. “Tea time. I did all the work.”

paragraphs.body: “We both did a lot of work. Sorry I was being nice to our client who, in case you haven’t noticed, gave us a hundred bucks.”
“I cleaned the garage. Really all the money should be mine, but I’ll be nice.”

Ant snatched one of the fifties and shoved it in his pocket. Hunter reached again for the volume knob, then left his hand over it so that when Ant reached for it, still looking at the road, he jerked back. They resumed an awkward silence for the rest of the trip.

annotations.comment: Very strong. I suspect you’ll eventually want to add something more here, about what Hunter is thinking. I especially wondered if Ant’s temper is new information, but in general, I loved this piece. Both boys seemed so real to me. I felt the client needed a bit more but she, too, is close to feeling real. Keep going! You’re on to something.
